subtopics.subtopic_title: BASELINE COMORBIDITY SCREENING AND ESCALATION CHECKPOINTS FOR TELE-OBESITY CARE
subtopics.category: TELE-NUTRITION PATHWAY FOR PEDIATRIC OBESITY AND METABOLIC RISK IN INDIA
subtopics.concept: Baseline comorbidity screening and escalation checkpoints are critical for effective tele-obesity care in paediatric populations, particularly in India. Tele-nutrition pathways must avoid overemphasis on diet logs at the expense of medical risk assessment. A minimum baseline dataset should be operationalized at enrollment or within 2–4 weeks if tele-first care is initiated. This dataset must include accurate height, weight, and BMI classification using paediatric references—WHO standards for children under 5 years and IAP growth charts for ages 5–18 years—with the reference method displayed on all reports to prevent caregiver confusion. Height measurement confidence is crucial, as errors can distort BMI for age, and clinic-measured baselines are preferred. Blood pressure should be recorded using an appropriately sized cuff, and clinic-measured waist circumference should be added when available. Additional components include pubertal context, a sleep screen for snoring or witnessed apneas, and a psychosocial screen for bullying, mood symptoms, and disordered eating risk. For children aged 10 years and older with obesity, baseline laboratory investigations should include fasting lipids, dysglycemia screening using fasting glucose or HbA1c, and ALT levels, with follow-up testing guided by abnormalities and clinical trajectory. Clear escalation triggers must be defined to ensure timely in-person assessment. These include symptoms of diabetes such as polyuria and polydipsia, suspected hypertension—especially stage 2 or symptomatic readings—sleep-disordered breathing with daytime impairment, rapid weight gain accompanied by edema or systemic symptoms, and positive eating disorder risk screens. Stabilization steps and thresholds for PICU admission are not specified in the source. To ensure effective care progression, implementation checkpoints should occur every 4–6 weeks to assess behavioral changes, with a 12-week trajectory checkpoint prompting pediatrician-led review and completion of comorbidity workup if required. Discharge and review triggers are not specified in the source. By adhering to these guidelines, tele-obesity care can integrate medical risk assessment with behavioral interventions, improving outcomes for paediatric patients.

case_studies.title: Tele-Nutrition Screening in a 12-Year-Old with Rapid Weight Gain and Fatigue
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old girl from a semi-urban area in India is enrolled in a tele-obesity care program after her parents report concern about her weight gain over the past 6 months. Her mother mentions that her school uniform no longer fits, and she has been more fatigued lately, often skipping physical activities she previously enjoyed. During the initial tele-screening, her weight is reported as 62 kg and height as 145 cm, but the caregiver admits difficulty measuring height accurately. Her BMI is calculated as 29.5 kg/m², placing her above the 95th percentile for age and sex on the IAP growth chart. Her mother also reports occasional snoring and daytime drowsiness. A psychosocial screen reveals that she has started avoiding school due to teasing from peers. No polyuria, polydipsia, or systemic symptoms are reported. A clinic visit is recommended for accurate height measurement, blood pressure assessment, and waist circumference recording. Baseline labs, including fasting lipids, fasting glucose, and ALT, are pending. The tele-nutrition team flags her case for escalation due to rapid weight gain, fatigue, and possible sleep-disordered breathing.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the importance of a baseline comorbidity screen and escalation triggers, including accurate anthropometric measurements, psychosocial risk factors, and symptoms suggestive of sleep-disordered breathing in tele-obesity care.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ADOLESCENT ANTI-STIGMA COUNSELING AND EATING DISORDER RISK SCREENING
subtopics.category: TELE-NUTRITION PATHWAY FOR PEDIATRIC OBESITY AND METABOLIC RISK IN INDIA
subtopics.concept: Adolescent anti-stigma counseling and eating disorder risk screening are critical components of tele-nutrition pathways, particularly in the context of pediatric care in India. Adolescents (<18 years) require specialized attention due to the intersection of confidentiality concerns, stigma, and eating disorder risks in digital communication. Indian telemedicine guidelines mandate parent, adult sibling, or legal guardian consent for minors, but it is essential to define how adolescent privacy is respected within this framework. A practical approach involves documenting guardian consent and participants while offering a brief confidential adolescent check-in, when safe and feasible, with clear boundaries regarding mandatory disclosures for safety. Tele weight-management tools, however, can inadvertently trigger restrictive eating behaviors, and child data protection norms prohibit tracking, behavioral monitoring, and targeted advertising directed at children. This underscores the importance of avoiding consumer calorie-counting apps and ad-tech platforms for adolescents. Screening for eating disorder risk should be integrated into all adolescent weight-focused tele-nutrition pathways. The SCOFF questionnaire, a validated five-item screening tool, is recommended for this purpose, with a score of ≥2 necessitating further evaluation. Tele-nutrition services must also establish clear criteria for urgent referrals rather than continuing remote coaching. These triggers include syncope, severe dietary restriction, rapid weight loss, purging behaviors, bradycardia symptoms, severe anxiety or depression, and suicidal ideation. If any of these risks are identified, the focus must shift immediately from weight-loss goals to medical safety, including in-person vitals assessment (with orthostatic measures) and coordinated multidisciplinary care. For example, if a 15-year-old requests tele-nutrition for “fitness” and shares rapid weight loss graphs with a SCOFF score of 3/5, calorie targets should not be prescribed. Instead, escalation to an in-person medical and mental health assessment is necessary, with counseling centered on safety, regular meals, and stigma-free support. To improve bedside decision-making, tele-nutrition notes should include mandatory fields for the SCOFF score and documentation of a private adolescent check-in, with automatic escalation prompts for positive findings. This structured approach ensures adolescent safety while addressing stigma and eating disorder risks effectively.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ANEMIA SEVERITY CLASSIFICATION, FOLLOW-UP SCHEDULING, AND NON-RESPONSE ESCALATION
subtopics.category: TELE-APPROACH TO ANEMIA AND MICRONUTRIENT DEFICIENCIES WITH SAFE SUPPLEMENTATION
subtopics.concept:
Anemia severity classification, follow-up scheduling, and non-response escalation in paediatric patients require structured protocols to ensure safe and effective management, particularly in tele-nutrition settings. Admission and escalation criteria are anchored to clinical symptoms and laboratory thresholds. Children presenting with signs of cardiopulmonary compromise, such as tachycardia at rest, breathlessness, syncope, or marked lethargy, necessitate urgent in-person assessment rather than remote management. For severe anemia, defined as hemoglobin <7 g/dL in young children, immediate referral is mandatory. Facility-level transfusion thresholds include hemoglobin ≤4 g/dL or 4–6 g/dL accompanied by shock, impaired consciousness, heart failure, deep/labored breathing, or dehydration. These cases are unsuitable for remote management and require escalation to higher-level care. Initial laboratory evaluation and stabilization steps are not specified in the source. For children aged 6 months to 5 years with mild or moderate anemia and clinical stability, the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW) recommends daily elemental iron supplementation at a dose of 3 mg/kg/day for two months. Follow-up should occur every 14 days to monitor adherence, tolerance, and side effects, with repeat hemoglobin testing scheduled after completing the treatment course. Tele follow-up documentation must include the prescribed dose, formulation, adherence barriers, side effects, and the date for repeat testing. Iron supplementation should be withheld or individualized under physician supervision in cases of acute illness, severe acute malnutrition (SAM), or known hemoglobinopathies, which are considered telemedicine “stop rules.”

Non-response to treatment after two months, despite adequate dosing and adherence, warrants escalation for further investigation. Potential underlying causes include hemoglobinopathies, chronic inflammation or infection, malabsorption syndromes such as celiac disease, or ongoing blood loss. To streamline care, an anemia follow-up template should incorporate a fixed 14-day review interval and an automated reminder for repeat hemoglobin testing at two months. Discharge and review triggers are not specified in the source.

case_studies.title: Tele-Nutrition in Pediatric Anemia: Escalation and Follow-Up Challenges
case_studies.vignette: A 3-year-old girl from a rural village is brought to a tele-nutrition consultation by her mother, who reports persistent fatigue and reduced appetite over the past month. The child has no history of fever, diarrhea, or other acute infections. Her initial hemoglobin level, tested at a local clinic, was 6.8 g/dL. She was started on daily oral iron supplementation (3 mg/kg/day) two weeks ago. During the follow-up video call, the mother mentions that the child remains lethargic and struggles to participate in daily activities. She also describes occasional breathlessness after minimal exertion. On visual inspection via telemedicine, the child appears pale but is alert. The mother denies any adherence issues with the iron regimen. No recent hemoglobin testing has been done, and the family lacks access to nearby diagnostic facilities. The tele-nutrition provider must decide whether the child’s condition warrants immediate escalation to in-person care or continued remote monitoring under structured follow-up.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MICROCYTIC ANEMIA DIFFERENTIALS AND HEMOGLOBINOPATHY SCREENING STOP RULES
subtopics.category: TELE-APPROACH TO ANEMIA AND MICRONUTRIENT DEFICIENCIES WITH SAFE SUPPLEMENTATION
subtopics.concept: Microcytic anemia in paediatric patients requires careful differential diagnosis and structured screening for hemoglobinopathies, particularly in regions like India where mixed anemia and hemoglobinopathies are prevalent. Tele-follow-up for anemia can enhance adherence to treatment and facilitate timely testing; however, it becomes unsafe if microcytosis is automatically equated with iron deficiency. To ensure safe remote management, tele-nutrition protocols must incorporate interpretation guardrails before escalating iron supplementation. The evaluation process should begin with age-specific and severity classifications using locked hemoglobin cutoff tables, distinguishing programmatic thresholds (e.g., Ministry of Health and Family Welfare [MoHFW] guidelines) from reference cutoffs like those provided by the WHO, especially if comparability is required. Any signs of cardiopulmonary compromise or hemoglobin levels in severe ranges necessitate immediate in-person evaluation rather than remote management. When indices are available, they should guide the differential diagnosis. Iron deficiency anemia typically presents with low mean corpuscular volume (MCV) and increased red cell distribution width (RDW), while thalassemia trait is characterized by disproportionate microcytosis and a relatively higher red blood cell (RBC) count for the degree of anemia. The Mentzer index, calculated as MCV divided by RBC count, can aid in screening: a value <13 suggests thalassemia trait, whereas a value ≥13 is indicative of iron deficiency. However, this index is a preliminary tool and does not replace confirmatory testing. In cases of iron deficiency anemia, a hemoglobin rise of approximately 1 g/dL within 14 days is expected following iron therapy. Failure to achieve this response should prompt a review of adherence and evaluation for alternative causes such as blood loss, malabsorption, inflammation, or other diagnoses, rather than prolonged or repeated iron supplementation. To optimize bedside decision-making, anemia follow-up templates should include mandatory fields for reviewing indices or noting their unavailability, alongside a non-response escalation pathway. These measures ensure comprehensive evaluation and safe management. Guidelines such as the MoHFW/NHM (2013) and WHO recommendations provide essential frameworks for managing pediatric anemia and should be integrated into clinical practice.

subtopics.subtopic_title: TELE-SAFE SUPPLEMENTATION POLICIES WITH DOSING, MONITORING, AND STOP RULES
subtopics.category: TELE-APPROACH TO ANEMIA AND MICRONUTRIENT DEFICIENCIES WITH SAFE SUPPLEMENTATION
subtopics.concept: Tele-safe supplementation policies for paediatric anemia and micronutrient deficiencies emphasize precise dosing, monitoring, and stop rules to ensure safety and efficacy during tele-nutrition consultations. According to Telemedicine Practice Guidelines, prescribing decisions are the responsibility of registered medical practitioners (RMPs), with medication categories restricted by the type and modality of consultation. Tele-nutrition services must establish written policies distinguishing prevention schedule verification and adherence reinforcement, often managed by counselors or dietitians, from therapeutic dosing and monitoring, which require RMP authorization with defined follow-up dates and stop rules. For therapeutic iron supplementation in mild to moderate anemia in young children, the recommended dose is 3 mg/kg/day of elemental iron for two months, with follow-ups every 14 days as per Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW) guidelines. Vitamin D prophylaxis in infancy, based on Indian Academy of Pediatrics (IAP) recommendations, involves administering 400 IU/day from the first days of life until one year, with treatment regimens requiring supervision to prevent toxicity. National Vitamin A prophylaxis, as per National Health Mission (NHM) guidelines, includes nine doses from 9 months to 5 years, with 100,000 IU at 9 months and 200,000 IU for children aged 1–5 years. Deworming should be integrated into anemia and growth pathways using NHM guidance, with albendazole dosed at 200 mg for children aged 1–2 years and 400 mg for those aged 2–19 years, while avoiding deworming during acute illness. Preterm supplementation must follow neonatal-specific protocols rather than ad hoc initiation. Enteral iron dosing for preterm or low birth weight infants typically ranges from 2–3 mg/kg/day, as per WHO evidence summaries, with timing and duration dependent on neonatal context and transfusion history. Tele-nutrition should align with neonatal follow-up plans in such cases. Practical implementation requires including an “RMP authorization: yes/no” field in therapeutic supplementation plans and embedding stop rules for acute illness, severe acute malnutrition (SAM), and non-response at predefined checkpoints.

case_studies.title: Tele-Nutrition and Safe Supplementation in a Young Child with Anemia
case_studies.vignette: A 2-year-old boy from a rural village in India is brought to a tele-nutrition consultation by his mother due to persistent fatigue, poor appetite, and pallor for the past month. The family reports no recent fever, diarrhea, or other acute illnesses. The child’s weight is 10 kg (below the 3rd percentile for age). His diet primarily consists of rice and lentils, with minimal intake of fruits, vegetables, or animal protein. During the consultation, the mother mentions that the child has not received any iron or vitamin supplementation in the past. A community health worker recently measured his hemoglobin using a point-of-care device, which showed a value of 9 g/dL. The tele-nutritionist notes the need for therapeutic supplementation and schedules a follow-up consultation in two weeks. The mother is counseled on adherence, dietary modifications, and potential side effects, and she expresses concern about how long the child will need supplementation.
case_studies.learning_objective: To assess the appropriate dosing, monitoring, and stop rules for therapeutic iron supplementation in mild to moderate anemia during tele-nutrition consultations, while ensuring patient safety and adherence to guidelines.

case_studies.title: Tele-Nutrition Challenges in Monitoring Iron Supplementation
case_studies.vignette: A 2-year-old boy from a rural village in India is enrolled in a tele-nutrition program after being diagnosed with mild anemia during a health camp. His mother reports that he has been looking pale and fatigued for the past two months. He weighs 12 kg, and his hemoglobin level at the camp was 9.5 g/dL. The tele-nutrition counselor initiated therapeutic iron supplementation at 3 mg/kg/day of elemental iron two weeks ago, as prescribed by the remote consulting pediatrician. During a follow-up teleconsultation, the mother mentions that the child has developed intermittent abdominal pain and dark-colored stools. She is unsure about continuing the medication and has missed giving a few doses due to concerns about side effects. The counselor escalates the case to the pediatrician for further advice. A focused history reveals no fever, vomiting, or diarrhea. The mother also reports that the child’s diet is predominantly rice-based with minimal vegetables or protein. No physical examination is possible due to the remote setting, but the pediatrician considers the need for adherence reinforcement, dose adjustment, or additional investigations.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand tele-safe supplementation policies, including dosing, monitoring for side effects, and implementing stop rules during tele-nutrition follow-ups for anemia management.

subtopics.subtopic_title: STRUCTURED TELE-ASSESSMENT OF INFANT FEEDING AND COMPLEMENTARY FEEDING ERRORS
subtopics.category: TELE-COUNSELLING WORKFLOWS FOR INFANT FEEDING AND COMMON DIETARY PROBLEMS
subtopics.concept: Structured tele-assessment of infant feeding and complementary feeding errors is a high-yield tele-nutrition approach, particularly effective in addressing technique-related issues through video observation and correction, often resulting in rapid improvements. A structured assessment is critical to ensure safety and avoid a narrow “feeding-only” focus, especially when illness or dehydration is present. Key domains to evaluate include the infant’s birth and neonatal history (gestation, birth weight, NICU stay), feeding method (exclusive breastfeeding, mixed feeding, formula preparation and dilution, and bottle hygiene), feeding frequency and duration, latch and positioning (preferably via video), output (urine frequency and stool pattern), vomiting or reflux patterns, developmental milestones, activity levels, and red flags such as fever, lethargy, respiratory distress, dehydration, poor suck, or aspiration events. In the context of complementary feeding, common errors in India include delayed introduction of solids, reliance on low-energy-density dilute porridges, inadequate protein diversity, overuse of juice or biscuits, force-feeding practices, and micronutrient-poor diets despite sufficient caloric intake. Counseling should be culturally sensitive and focus on practical, repeatable changes that caregivers can implement daily. For bedside decision-making, if the infant is stable and hydration is adequate, the priority is to correct high-impact technique errors such as latch, feeding frequency, formula dilution, or energy density. Early follow-up is essential to confirm implementation. If growth does not improve after two structured follow-ups or if red flags emerge, further evaluation for organic diseases or feeding-skill disorders should be initiated rather than relying on repeated formula changes via remote consultation. A practical tele-lactation protocol for infants with growth concerns should include three mandatory video assessments: one for latch and positioning, one for formula preparation if applicable, and one for a complementary feeding session if age-appropriate. This structured approach ensures comprehensive evaluation and targeted intervention, aligning with WHO guidelines for complementary feeding in children aged 6–23 months.

subtopics.subtopic_title: REMOTE DIET RECALLS, PHOTO AUDITS, AND BEHAVIOR CHANGE EXPERIMENT DESIGN
subtopics.category: TELE-COUNSELLING WORKFLOWS FOR INFANT FEEDING AND COMMON DIETARY PROBLEMS
subtopics.concept: Remote diet recalls, photo audits, and behavior change experiment design are critical components of tele-nutrition workflows for addressing infant feeding and common dietary problems in paediatric practice. The quality of diet recalls improves significantly when they are structured as clinical tools rather than open-ended conversations. Effective remote diet recalls include a weekday-plus-weekend recall, photo-based meal audits (capturing before and after images of plates), photographs of snack cupboards, grocery list reviews, and mapping of daily routines such as wake times, school schedules, tuition timings, and caregiver availability. Simplicity is essential; the most effective tools are those that families can use consistently, as overly complex logs tend to reduce adherence and increase dropout rates. In tele-nutrition, the cornerstone of behavior change is the implementation of short, testable “one-week experiments” with a clear measurement plan. Data collected during recalls and audits should be translated into a small number of high-impact dietary changes. Examples include replacing one sugar-sweetened beverage daily with water or unsweetened milk, adding one planned protein snack after school, shifting dinner earlier by 30 minutes, or incorporating an energy-dense addition (such as oil, ghee, or nut paste, where safe) into a toddler’s meal. Each dietary change should be paired with a specific observation request, such as submitting two meal photos, one scale photo, or a checklist, and a fixed follow-up date should be established. A clinical vignette illustrates the utility of photo audits: a 9-year-old with obesity reported consuming “home food only,” but photo evidence revealed daily consumption of packaged snacks and sweetened drinks that the family did not classify as “food.” Identifying these discrepancies allowed for the creation of specific, feasible dietary targets. To enhance follow-up, a copy-ready message template can be used, outlining the week’s goal, required submissions (e.g., photos or weight), red flags to monitor, and the next scheduled call. This message should be documented in the medical record the same day to facilitate team audits of requests and responses.

case_studies.title: Remote Diet Recall and Photo Audit for a 7-Year-Old with Poor Weight Gain
case_studies.vignette: A 7-year-old boy from a semi-urban area in India presents for a tele-nutrition consultation due to poor weight gain over the past year. His mother reports that he is a "picky eater" and primarily consumes home-cooked meals. She denies frequent intake of junk food but mentions that he often skips breakfast and prefers snacks like biscuits after school. His daily routine includes school from 8 AM to 2 PM, followed by tuition classes until 5 PM. A weekday-plus-weekend diet recall is conducted remotely, and the family is asked to submit photo audits of his meals, snack cupboard, and grocery list. The photos reveal a reliance on low-calorie snacks (e.g., biscuits, puffed rice), minimal protein sources, and delayed dinner times (9 PM or later). His mother is hesitant to make changes, citing time constraints during the school week. Physical examination, conducted during a prior visit, showed mild thinness (BMI-for-age in the 3rd–5th percentile) but no signs of systemic illness. The family is asked to test a one-week experiment of adding a protein-rich snack (e.g., boiled egg or paneer) after school and shifting dinner to 8 PM, with follow-up via meal photos and a checklist.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the use of structured remote diet recalls, photo audits, and short-term behavior change experiments to identify and address dietary gaps in a child with poor weight gain.

subtopics.subtopic_title: PORTION ESTIMATION USING INDIAN HOUSEHOLD MEASURES AND PHOTO-ASSISTED RECALLS
subtopics.category: TELE-COUNSELLING WORKFLOWS FOR INFANT FEEDING AND COMMON DIETARY PROBLEMS
subtopics.concept: Portion estimation using Indian household measures and photo-assisted recalls is a practical approach to enhance the effectiveness of diet counseling in paediatric tele-nutrition workflows. In the context of Indian diets, gram-level precision is often unrealistic due to the prevalence of mixed dishes, shared plates, and variable oil usage. Instead, a standardized two-step method is recommended. The first step involves using household measures such as katori/bowl, tablespoon, ladle, roti diameter count, cup, and “handful” for snacks, recorded as counts per day rather than calorie values. The second step incorporates photo-assisted calibration, where caregivers are asked to provide a photo of a typical meal placed next to a known object, such as a standard spoon or matchbox-sized reference, along with a brief description of recipe additions like oil, sugar, and ghee. Over time, this process helps create a family-specific portion dictionary, allowing follow-ups to reliably track dietary changes. For mixed dishes, three standard questions should be asked: identifying the main cereal base, the primary protein source, and the amount of visible oil or fat added. For meals consumed outside the home, including school meals, portion sizes should be compared to home meals (smaller, same, or larger), with weekend variations captured separately. High-yield hidden energy sources such as oil/ghee added during cooking, sweetened beverages, packaged snacks, and frequent “health drinks” should be documented. In cases of paediatric undernutrition, the focus should be on culturally acceptable and safe energy-dense additions, such as oil/ghee, nut paste (when age-appropriate), milk, curd, eggs, and legumes, while ensuring affordability. To implement this method effectively, it is essential to include a “portion method used” field in dietary records, specifying whether household measures alone or photo-calibrated estimates were applied. Calorie targets should only be prescribed if deemed safe and feasible by both a dietitian and a registered medical practitioner (RMP). This approach aligns with guidelines from the Indian Food Composition Tables (ICMR-NIN, 2017) and complementary feeding recommendations for infants and young children aged 6–23 months (WHO, 2023).

case_studies.title: Portion Estimation in a Malnourished Toddler Using Tele-Nutrition Tools
case_studies.vignette: A 2-year-old boy from a semi-urban area in India is brought for a tele-nutrition consultation due to persistent low weight despite regular meals. His mother reports he eats home-cooked food, including rice, dal, vegetables, and chapati, but she struggles to estimate his portion sizes. Over the past 3 months, his weight has plateaued at 8.5 kg (below the 3rd percentile for age). His mother shares photos of his meals during the consultation, placing the food next to a standard tablespoon and a small steel katori. She describes recipes, noting that she uses minimal oil and no ghee due to cost concerns. School snacks are infrequent, but he occasionally has biscuits or sweetened milk. On focused questioning, she mentions the child often refuses food and prefers liquids. The caregiver is also asked to compare his school meal portions to home meals, which she states are smaller. The family struggles with affordability but is open to suggestions for energy-dense additions. No fever or acute symptoms are reported. Examination during a prior in-person visit showed mild wasting but no edema or systemic findings.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CELIAC DISEASE ADHERENCE AND RELAPSE RISK TELE-CHECKLIST
subtopics.category: DISEASE-SPECIFIC TELE-CHECKLISTS FOR CHRONIC DISEASE AND NEURODISABILITY
subtopics.concept: The management of celiac disease in paediatric patients relies on strict adherence to a gluten-free diet and structured follow-up, making it well-suited for tele-nutrition when clear escalation protocols are in place. Expected defervescence timelines are not specified in the source. Treatment failure is suspected when persistent symptoms occur despite reported adherence, when growth faltering continues, or when anemia does not respond to appropriate therapy. A stepwise work-up during tele-visits should begin by confirming the diagnostic context, distinguishing between biopsy-confirmed and presumptive cases, and identifying any comorbid autoimmune conditions. It is also essential to assess the family’s understanding of the lifelong commitment to a gluten-free diet. A comprehensive tele-checklist should include four key areas: exposure risks, symptoms, growth, and micronutrient status. The exposure review must evaluate hidden gluten sources, such as spices, sauces, processed snacks, cross-contamination risks in shared kitchens, school meal exposures, and the family’s label-reading competence. Symptom screening should encompass diarrhea, abdominal pain, bloating, weight loss, fatigue, and behavioral changes, as relapse can present subtly. Growth assessment should focus on trajectory rather than isolated measures, with growth faltering triggering further evaluation if centile crossing thresholds are met. Micronutrient and anemia risks should be reviewed, particularly for signs of iron deficiency and dietary iron adequacy. Laboratory follow-up, if part of local practice, may include serology such as tissue transglutaminase IgA (tTG IgA) with total IgA as a first-line approach, though interpretation depends on timing, adherence, and individual context. Therapy adjustments, such as switching or extending treatment, are not specified in the source. Follow-up should include explicit escalation triggers, such as persistent symptoms, growth faltering, or unresolved anemia, which necessitate paediatric gastroenterology referral to reconsider gluten exposures, alternative diagnoses, or complications. A structured follow-up template should include mandatory fields for exposure risks, symptom screening, growth trajectory, and escalation needs.

subtopics.subtopic_title: TYPE 1 DIABETES SICK-DAY NUTRITION AND KETOSIS PREVENTION TELE-CHECKLIST
subtopics.category: DISEASE-SPECIFIC TELE-CHECKLISTS FOR CHRONIC DISEASE AND NEURODISABILITY
subtopics.concept:
Effective management of type 1 diabetes during illness requires a structured sick-day nutrition and ketosis prevention plan, particularly in paediatric patients. Tele-nutrition interventions must align with the child’s established diabetes care plan and avoid contradicting insulin adjustment guidance provided by the treating diabetes team. The primary goal during sick days is to prevent ketosis by maintaining adequate carbohydrate intake, ensuring hydration, and avoiding insulin omission. A telemedicine sick-day checklist should systematically assess key factors, including the child’s current illness symptoms such as fever, vomiting, or diarrhea; their ability to tolerate oral fluids and carbohydrates; current insulin doses and any missed doses; the frequency of blood glucose monitoring; and the ketone monitoring plan, whether via blood or urine, as specified in the child’s diabetes protocol.

Nutrition recommendations during illness should focus on practical, actionable strategies. Small, frequent amounts of carbohydrate-containing fluids or foods should be offered, such as oral rehydration solutions with added carbohydrates, soups with starch, milk, yogurt, or glucose-containing fluids, depending on the child’s specific plan. Prolonged fasting must be avoided, and basal insulin should be continued unless explicitly advised otherwise by the diabetes care team. Clear escalation triggers must be identified and documented for timely intervention. Warning signs such as vomiting with positive ketones, dehydration, rapid breathing, abdominal pain, altered sensorium, or an inability to maintain carbohydrate intake indicate a high risk of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) and necessitate urgent in-person evaluation. Families should be instructed not to delay carbohydrate intake, even if the child lacks appetite, when ketones are present.

To enhance bedside decision-making, a one-page sick-day handout should be made available on the telemedicine platform. This handout should include a list of preferred carbohydrate-containing fluids, the minimum frequency of monitoring based on the child’s diabetes plan, and emergency contact details along with referral instructions for urgent care. These measures are supported by the ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines for sick-day management in children and adolescents with diabetes.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE ELECTROLYTE RISK AND DIET SAFETY TELE-CHECKLIST
subtopics.category: DISEASE-SPECIFIC TELE-CHECKLISTS FOR CHRONIC DISEASE AND NEURODISABILITY
subtopics.concept:
Chronic kidney disease (CKD) in paediatric patients presents significant risks related to electrolyte disturbances, particularly hyperkalemia, which can be life-threatening if dietary advice is given without proper context. Tele-nutrition for CKD must be guided by a structured checklist to ensure safety and precision in dietary recommendations. This checklist should begin with a review of the renal context, including the CKD stage, etiology (if known), dialysis status, recent serum potassium and bicarbonate levels (or confirmation if laboratory results are unavailable), and current medications that may influence potassium levels, as reviewed by the physician. Dietary assessment should focus on identifying high-potassium food sources, such as bananas, coconut water, certain lentils, tomato-based gravies, salt substitutes, packaged foods, and cultural staples. However, dietary advice should be tailored to the child’s laboratory trends rather than relying on a universal “avoid list.” 

Safety checks during tele-nutrition consultations are critical. Symptoms such as weakness, palpitations, paresthesias, severe constipation, or acute illness with reduced urine output may indicate hyperkalemia or fluid/electrolyte instability and necessitate urgent medical evaluation rather than dietary adjustments alone. If laboratory tests are overdue in a child with changing dietary intake, dehydration, vomiting, diarrhea, or recent medication changes, in-person assessment and updated blood tests should be scheduled before implementing stricter dietary restrictions. Interim guidance should include avoiding very high-potassium “bolus” items and salt substitutes while arranging updated laboratory evaluations. If potassium levels are confirmed to be elevated or symptoms strongly suggest hyperkalemia, the case should be escalated urgently to the treating nephrology or paediatric team. To ensure safe practice, every CKD tele-nutrition note should include a “recent potassium available: yes/no” field, and closure of the consultation should be blocked if no laboratory plan is documented. This approach emphasizes conservative management while prioritizing laboratory monitoring and timely escalation to specialist care when necessary.

case_studies.title: Tele-Nutrition Safety in a Child with CKD and Suspected Electrolyte Imbalance
case_studies.vignette:
A 12-year-old boy from a rural area in India with Stage 3 chronic kidney disease (CKD) secondary to posterior urethral valves presents with complaints of fatigue, muscle weakness, and occasional palpitations over the past week. His mother reports recent dietary changes, including increased consumption of bananas and lentil-based dishes after a neighbor suggested they were 'energy-boosting.' He is not on dialysis and has missed his last follow-up appointment for blood tests. His current medications include an ACE inhibitor. No recent vomiting or diarrhea is reported, but his urine output has decreased slightly. On teleconsultation, the child appears alert but tired, with no acute distress. The mother is unsure of his last serum potassium or bicarbonate levels. She also mentions difficulty accessing a nearby lab for tests. The family is seeking guidance on whether to adjust his diet or seek in-person care. 

What is your next step in evaluating and managing this patient via tele-nutrition?

subtopics.subtopic_title: CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE FEEDING FATIGUE AND GROWTH SAFETY TELE-CHECKLIST
subtopics.category: DISEASE-SPECIFIC TELE-CHECKLISTS FOR CHRONIC DISEASE AND NEURODISABILITY
subtopics.concept: Feeding fatigue and growth safety in infants with congenital heart disease require careful monitoring and structured nutritional strategies, particularly when using tele-nutrition tools. Unlike standard calorie-focused approaches, nutrition care in these patients prioritizes feeding endurance and minimizing cardiopulmonary workload. Tele-nutrition is valuable for maintaining care continuity and providing caregiver coaching, but it must incorporate a comprehensive safety checklist to identify feeding fatigue and symptoms of heart failure. Infants with congenital heart disease often benefit from smaller, more frequent feeds due to their tendency to tire quickly. Caregivers should be explicitly guided to prioritize feeding frequency over large volume feeds, as prolonged or forced feeding can increase aspiration risk. A practical tele-checklist should include key parameters such as feeding frequency, total daily volume, duration of each feed, signs of fatigue (e.g., falling asleep early or prolonged feeding sessions), sweating or breathlessness during feeds, tachypnea at rest, recurrent vomiting, poor urine output, and growth trajectory. It is crucial to document the feeding method—whether breast, bottle, or tube—as each poses unique risks and requires tailored coaching. Escalation triggers must be clearly defined, including respiratory distress, cyanosis, suspected heart failure symptoms, poor growth despite structured feeding plans, or feeds consistently taking excessive time with associated distress. These signs warrant immediate in-person evaluation and coordination with cardiology. While tele-nutrition can provide interim strategies—such as shorter, more frequent feeds, pacing, rest breaks, and increasing energy density within safe volumes—medical optimization should never be delayed. For example, a caregiver reporting a “picky feeder” may require further assessment if video observation reveals sweating and pauses to breathe during prolonged bottle feeds. Such cases indicate potential cardiology escalation rather than behavioral feeding issues. To enhance bedside decision-making, two mandatory questions should be added to all congenital heart disease follow-ups: “How long does one feed take?” and “Any sweating or breathlessness during feeds?” These questions ensure early identification of feeding fatigue and potential complications, allowing timely intervention to optimize growth and safety outcomes.

case_studies.vignette: A 3-month-old infant with known congenital heart disease is being monitored through a tele-nutrition program in a semi-urban Indian setting. The mother reports that the baby has been feeding poorly over the past 3 days, requiring longer feeding sessions (45–50 minutes per feed) and falling asleep midway. She also notes that the baby sweats excessively during feeds and appears breathless at rest, with a respiratory rate of 60 breaths per minute. The baby has been exclusively bottle-fed with expressed breast milk, but the mother has struggled to maintain frequent feeds due to the baby's fatigue. Over the past week, the baby has had decreased urine output and has vomited after feeds on two occasions. A virtual review of the tele-checklist reveals prolonged feeding sessions, reduced total daily feed volume, and poor weight gain (weight stagnant at 4.2 kg from last month). The caregiver expresses concern about worsening symptoms and asks whether the baby needs hospital care.

subtopics.subtopic_title: NEURODISABILITY ASPIRATION RISK AND CAREGIVER BURDEN TELE-CHECKLIST
subtopics.category: DISEASE-SPECIFIC TELE-CHECKLISTS FOR CHRONIC DISEASE AND NEURODISABILITY
subtopics.concept:
Children with neurodisabilities, such as cerebral palsy, frequently face significant feeding safety challenges and an elevated caregiver burden, necessitating careful assessment of aspiration risk during tele-nutrition consultations before focusing on calorie escalation. Aspiration risk and caregiver burden should be systematically evaluated using a structured tele-checklist at every follow-up, as these factors can fluctuate with illness or growth. Feeding safety screening must include inquiries about coughing or choking during feeds, a wet or gurgly voice after swallowing, recurrent chest infections or pneumonia, increased work of breathing during meals, prolonged feeding times, drooling, and difficulty coordinating chewing and swallowing. Additional questions should cover texture modifications already in use, feeding positions, and whether caregivers are forcing thin liquids despite choking episodes. A video recording of a typical feeding session can provide critical insights into unsafe feeding patterns that caregivers may not articulate clearly.

Caregiver burden is an essential clinical variable to assess. Clinicians should document the duration of a single meal, the total hours spent feeding daily, and whether the caregiver can sustain the feeding plan without experiencing burnout. Overly complex or time-intensive feeding regimens can lead to caregiver dropout, potentially exacerbating the child’s nutritional challenges if the plan is abandoned. If aspiration risk is suspected, the priority should be safety: caregivers should pause unsafe textures, and referrals should be made for in-person swallow evaluations or speech and swallow therapy, alongside coordination of medical reviews. If feeding safety is stable, tele-nutrition can shift focus to enhancing energy density, advancing texture progression, and reducing meal duration by improving feeding efficiency. Persistent weight faltering despite safe feeding strategies warrants multidisciplinary review and consideration of enteral feeding options in collaboration with the treating team.

To streamline care, two mandatory questions should be added to every neurodisability tele-visit: “Any choking/coughing during feeds?” and “How long does one meal take?” Positive responses should trigger an automatic referral for swallow assessment to ensure timely intervention.

case_studies.title: Tele-Nutrition Checklist for Aspiration Risk in a Child with Cerebral Palsy
case_studies.vignette: A 6-year-old boy with spastic quadriplegic cerebral palsy is brought to a tele-nutrition follow-up by his mother from a rural village in India. His mother reports increasing difficulty during mealtimes over the past two months. She notes frequent coughing during feeds, a gurgly voice after swallowing, and two episodes of fever treated at home in the last month. Feeding takes over 90 minutes per meal, and she spends nearly six hours daily trying to feed him. Despite her efforts, he has lost 1 kg in the last three months. The child is fed semi-solid textures, but his mother admits to occasionally forcing thin liquids despite observing choking episodes. She reports feeling overwhelmed and exhausted, struggling to balance caregiving with household responsibilities. A video recording provided during the consultation shows the child drooling excessively, coughing intermittently, and appearing fatigued during meals. On focused questioning, she confirms that he is fed in a reclined position due to difficulty sitting upright. No recent hospitalizations or invasive interventions have been required.
case_studies.learning_objective: Evaluate aspiration risk and caregiver burden using a structured tele-checklist to determine safe feeding practices and triage needs in children with neurodisabilities.

subtopics.subtopic_title: BOOKING TO OUTCOME TRACKING WORKFLOW WITH RESPONSIBILITY HANDOFFS
subtopics.category: END-TO-END TELE-NUTRITION SERVICE WORKFLOW, REGISTRIES, AND DASHBOARDS
subtopics.concept: In paediatric tele-nutrition services, the "Booking to Outcome Tracking Workflow with Responsibility Handoffs" is a structured, multi-step operational pipeline designed to ensure comprehensive care and accountability. This workflow begins with Step 1: booking, pathway assignment, and consent capture. During this phase, staff document whether the consultation was initiated with implied or explicit consent, verify guardian authorization for minors, and assign the case to an appropriate pathway (e.g., growth faltering, undernutrition, obesity, anemia, or chronic disease) using a brief screening script. Step 2 involves measurement coaching and structured data collection prior to clinician involvement. Caregivers are coached on techniques and asked to provide specific artifacts, such as scale photos, meal photos from specified days, and lab report photos. Step 3 is clinician review, where the supervising clinician or registered medical practitioner (RMP) evaluates tele-appropriateness, classifies the child’s risk tier, sets response targets, documents stop rules, and schedules a follow-up date. High-risk cases—such as suspected severe acute malnutrition (SAM) with danger signs, severe anemia symptoms, or growth concerns in children under six months with poor data—should default to referral and safety-netting rather than prolonged remote counseling. Step 4 focuses on delivering the care plan in a caregiver-usable format, combining structured text with a brief voice note for those with limited literacy. The plan must be documented in the medical record rather than solely in messaging threads. Step 5 involves follow-up reminders and registry updates, where each plan generates a registry entry that includes the next due date, measurement confidence, and escalation triggers. Registries are reviewed weekly to prioritize overdue high-risk cases. Step 6 is outcome checkpoint documentation at 2–4 weeks, categorizing outcomes as improved, unchanged, worsened, or lost to follow-up, thereby creating a minimum dataset for quality improvement. To ensure compliance, a “note cannot be closed” rule should be implemented unless fields for consent, guardian authorization, risk tier, and next follow-up date are completed. This structured workflow ensures continuity of care and facilitates quality monitoring in paediatric tele-nutrition services.

subtopics.subtopic_title: COPY-READY TELE-TRIAGE SCRIPT AND PATHWAY-SPECIFIC INTAKE DATA FIELDS
subtopics.category: END-TO-END TELE-NUTRITION SERVICE WORKFLOW, REGISTRIES, AND DASHBOARDS
subtopics.concept: Structured intake using a standardized tele-triage script and pathway-specific intake fields is critical in reducing unsafe variability and ensuring auditable, team-delivered tele-nutrition services for paediatric patients. Admission and escalation criteria are assessed through a copy-ready triage script, which must be asked verbatim by coordinators or nurses before clinician involvement. The script includes the following questions: “Is the child breathing fast or with difficulty?”, “Is the child unusually sleepy, difficult to wake, or has had seizures?”, “Is the child able to drink/feed?”, “Any repeated vomiting?”, “Any blood in stool or severe diarrhea?”, “Any swelling of both feet?”, “Any fever lasting more than 7 days?”, and “Any choking/coughing during feeds?”. A “yes” to any of these questions flags the child for urgent clinician review, with referral instructions provided as per protocol. Initial labs and intake data are collected asynchronously and tailored to specific pathways. For growth concerns, data must include the last three weights with dates, details of who measured them, a photo of the scale, the length/height measurement method, and a 24-hour diet snapshot. For acute malnutrition, the required fields include MUAC (for ages 6–59 months only), appetite status, diarrhea/vomiting screening, and program linkage status. Obesity-related intake focuses on beverage/snack patterns, sleep routines, activity constraints, and a psychosocial screen. Anemia-specific data include hemoglobin value and date, iron dosing history and tolerance, and red-flag symptoms. For chronic diseases, a condition-specific checklist and recent key labs are required. Stabilization steps emphasize equity by offering the script in the caregiver’s preferred language and allowing audio completion when literacy or connectivity challenges exist. Thresholds for PICU admission are not specified in the source. Discharge and review triggers are automated through a one-page digital form that auto-populates the telenote, assigns the appropriate pathway and risk tier, and schedules the next due date before the clinician call begins. This structured approach ensures comprehensive and efficient tele-nutrition care for paediatric patients.

MUAC measurement is unavailable, and the child’s appetite is described as poor. The tele-nutrition team flags the case for urgent clinician review and advises the mother to prepare for further in-person evaluation. subtopics.subtopic_title: FOLLOW-UP REMINDER SYSTEM WITH MISSED-VISIT ESCALATION AND CLOSED-LOOP VERIFICATION
subtopics.category: END-TO-END TELE-NUTRITION SERVICE WORKFLOW, REGISTRIES, AND DASHBOARDS
subtopics.concept: The follow-up reminder system with missed-visit escalation and closed-loop verification is a critical safety feature in tele-nutrition services, ensuring continuity of care between consultations. Admission and escalation criteria are not specified in the source. Follow-up pathways must be tailored to specific conditions, with clearly defined cadences and checkpoints. For example, growth faltering requires a two-week checkpoint and a four-week trajectory review, while acute malnutrition necessitates weekly or fortnightly weight and MUAC checks depending on risk. Anemia management involves an early adherence check and a hemoglobin response assessment within the expected window, and obesity care includes a four-to-six-week behavior implementation checkpoint followed by a twelve-week trajectory review. Reminders should specify the required artifact, such as a weight photo, MUAC value, lab report, or meal photo, along with a due date. Initial labs are not specified in the source. Stabilization steps include operationalizing missed follow-ups by sending a first reminder within 24–48 hours after a missed due date, attempting a phone call if there is no response, and documenting each attempt. Caregiver confirmation is required, including a “done” response and attachment, to ensure reminders are not one-way communication. Repeated failed contacts should lead to reclassification as “lost to follow-up,” triggering alternative outreach or program linkage if the risk is high. For obesity and adolescent care, non-stigmatizing language should be used, and sensitive details must be avoided in push notifications on shared phones. When follow-up necessitates an in-person visit or lab, an escalation verification task must be added to confirm completion rather than assuming it occurred. Thresholds for PICU admission are not specified in the source. Discharge and review triggers are similarly not provided. To streamline operations, three standard reminder templates should be built—measurement due, lab due, and appointment due—and staff must close the loop by recording “data received and reviewed” or “escalation initiated.” This system strengthens tele-nutrition workflows by ensuring accountability and continuity of care.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MINIMUM VIABLE REGISTRY DATASET AND ACTIONABLE ALERT RULES
subtopics.category: END-TO-END TELE-NUTRITION SERVICE WORKFLOW, REGISTRIES, AND DASHBOARDS
subtopics.concept: A clinically useful registry in paediatric tele-nutrition must drive actionable outcomes, such as recall, referral, escalation, or de-escalation. To achieve this, a minimum viable dataset should be defined as a data dictionary that allows dashboards to prioritize “risk + time.” Core identifiers in the dataset include unique child ID, name, age, sex, caregiver details (name and relationship), contact information, location, and preferred language. Essential clinical fields encompass the growth reference used (WHO, IAP 2015, or preterm charts), dated anthropometric measurements (weight, length/height, and MUAC, where relevant), derived indices (WAZ, HAZ, WHZ, BAZ), and syndrome flags such as faltering growth thresholds, acute malnutrition risk, obesity risk, and anemia suspicion. Measurement confidence must be explicitly categorized as high (clinic/device verified), moderate (coached home readings with repetition), or low (uncertain). Risk and escalation fields should include risk tier, presence of red flags (yes/no), escalation requirements (none, scheduled in-person visit, or urgent referral), and escalation completion details such as date and facility. Follow-up data should capture next due date, overdue status, missed contact count, preferred follow-up mode, assigned team member, and the “barrier of the week.” Audit fields must document consent, guardian verification for minors, media consent (if files are exchanged), plan version ID, and record retention category. Actionable alert rules should be concise and tied to specific interventions. For example, MUAC <115 mm in children aged 6–59 months or suspected oedema should trigger urgent referral and block routine closure. Faltering growth thresholds should initiate a 2-week checkpoint plan, while hemoglobin levels below severe thresholds (e.g., <7 g/dL in young children) necessitate urgent evaluation and halt routine iron supplementation without review by a registered medical practitioner (RMP). Repeated missed follow-ups in high-risk children should prompt active outreach. For immediate implementation, a spreadsheet registry using these fields can be created, with weekly reviews focusing on overdue high-risk children to ensure timely interventions.

subtopics.subtopic_title: DASHBOARD VIEWS FOR OVERDUE HIGH-RISK CHILDREN AND FAILED CHECKPOINTS
subtopics.category: END-TO-END TELE-NUTRITION SERVICE WORKFLOW, REGISTRIES, AND DASHBOARDS
subtopics.concept: Dashboard views for overdue high-risk children and failed checkpoints are essential tools for paediatric tele-nutrition workflows, enabling clinicians to address critical questions daily: who is overdue, who is worsening, and who requires escalation with verified completion. These dashboards should function as actionable lists rather than retrospective analytics, ensuring timely interventions. The first dashboard view, the high-risk overdue list, must allow filtering by risk tier, measurement confidence, and next due date, with sorting by days overdue. It should include features like one-click contact attempt logging, options for preferred follow-up modes (audio, video, or in-person), and a “failed contact count” field. This field should trigger alternative outreach strategies or linkage to community services for children who remain unreachable. The second view, the failed checkpoint list, focuses on identifying children who have not met specific improvement benchmarks. For growth faltering, it flags children without improvement at the 2-week or 4-week checkpoint. For acute malnutrition in children aged 6–59 months, it highlights lack of weight or mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) improvement at 2 weeks. For anemia, it flags cases where hemoglobin levels have not risen within the expected response window. For obesity, it identifies children with either a lack of behavioral implementation at 4–6 weeks or a worsening body mass index-for-age (BAZ) trajectory. Each flag should link to a structured escalation note template prompting symptom re-screening, measurement verification, and test or referral ordering through a registered medical practitioner (RMP) review. The third view, escalation verification, tracks whether in-person measurements, lab tests, or referral visits have occurred, documenting the facility and date. Unverified escalations represent a key telecare safety failure, and repeated failed contacts should result in a “lost to follow-up” status to maintain accurate denominators in quality metrics. To implement these dashboards immediately, clinicians can start with three spreadsheet tabs corresponding to these views and conduct weekly reviews focused solely on children appearing in any of the tabs. This approach ensures timely identification and management of high-risk paediatric cases.

case_studies.title: Dashboard Alert for Overdue High-Risk Child with Growth Faltering
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy from a rural village in India is flagged on the tele-nutrition dashboard as overdue for a high-risk follow-up by 10 days. He was enrolled in the program for moderate acute malnutrition (MUAC 11.2 cm) and growth faltering (weight-for-age Z-score -3.2) two months ago. At his last virtual checkpoint, his weight had plateaued, and his caregiver reported difficulty accessing supplementary nutrition packets due to local unavailability. No improvement was noted at the 2-week checkpoint. The dashboard now lists him under the failed checkpoint category for lack of weight gain at the 4-week mark. Attempts to contact his caregiver via phone have failed three times, as logged in the system. His flagged status prompts a structured escalation note for symptom re-screening and verification of MUAC and weight. The caregiver's preferred follow-up mode is listed as in-person, but no recent visit is recorded.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the utility of dashboard views in identifying high-risk children overdue for follow-up or failing checkpoints, and recognize when escalation strategies, including in-person assessments, are necessary.

case_studies.title: Dashboard Alert for Overdue High-Risk Child with Failed Checkpoints
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy from a rural community in India is flagged on the tele-nutrition dashboard as high-risk and overdue for follow-up by 10 days. He was previously identified with moderate acute malnutrition (MUAC: 11.5 cm) and started on therapeutic nutrition support. His 2-week checkpoint noted no improvement in MUAC, which remained at 11.5 cm, and his weight stagnated at 7.4 kg. The caregiver reported poor appetite and occasional diarrhea during the last teleconsultation. Despite three failed contact attempts logged on the dashboard, the family remains unreachable. The failed checkpoint list now flags this child due to lack of MUAC improvement and overdue status. A community health worker is dispatched to verify measurements and assess for acute symptoms. On in-person evaluation, the child is lethargic, with sunken eyes and delayed capillary refill. MUAC is re-measured at 11.4 cm, and weight is recorded at 7.2 kg. The caregiver mentions worsening diarrhea for the past three days.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the utility of tele-nutrition dashboards in identifying overdue high-risk children with failed checkpoints, and assess when escalation to in-person evaluation or acute care is warranted.

subtopics.subtopic_title: QUALITY METRICS AND MONTHLY AUDIT CYCLES FOR TELE-NUTRITION SERVICES
subtopics.category: END-TO-END TELE-NUTRITION SERVICE WORKFLOW, REGISTRIES, AND DASHBOARDS
subtopics.concept: Quality metrics and monthly audit cycles for paediatric tele-nutrition services should prioritize safe, longitudinal care over service volume. Metrics must include at least one structural measure, one process measure, and one outcome proxy, all reviewed within a predictable audit cycle. Structural metrics should ensure documentation completeness, including mandatory fields such as consent documentation, guardian verification for minors, screening for red flags, stating growth references, recording measurement confidence, and assigning the next follow-up date. Process metrics should assess whether care was effectively delivered, such as follow-up completion rates for high-risk children, the proportion of overdue high-risk children contacted within a defined window, and the time to escalation when red flags are detected remotely. Outcome proxies should be condition-specific and direction-based. For growth faltering, the trajectory should be classified as improved, unchanged, or worsened at four weeks. For undernutrition, early weight gain or improved mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) in children aged 6–59 months should be evaluated at a two-week checkpoint. For obesity, stabilization or slowing of BMI-for-age z-score (BAZ) trajectory and improved sleep/activity routines should be prioritized over rapid weight loss. Safety and equity must also be integral to audits. This includes tracking the proportion of visits where anthropometry techniques were verified using video or photo evidence and the proportion of families successfully managed through low-tech pathways, such as audio consultations supplemented by periodic in-person measurements. Satisfaction scores should not be the sole metric, as they may incentivize reassurance over necessary escalation. To operationalize these principles, a monthly 30-minute dashboard review is recommended. During this review, the top three failure modes—such as missed follow-ups, low measurement confidence, and delayed escalation—should be identified. Subsequently, one workflow fix should be implemented each month, followed by re-measurement to assess improvement. These practices align with WHO guidelines on monitoring and evaluating digital health interventions and provide a structured approach to enhancing tele-nutrition services for paediatric populations.

subtopics.subtopic_title: SERVICE LINKAGE PRESCRIPTIONS FOR NRC, ANGANWADI, AND SCHOOL AGE PROGRAMS
subtopics.category: PUBLIC PROGRAM LINKAGE AND SERVICE CONTINUITY IN INDIA
subtopics.concept: Service linkage prescriptions for Nutrition Rehabilitation Centres (NRCs), Anganwadi centers, and school-age programs are critical components of tele-nutrition in India, ensuring continuity of care across clinical, community, and educational settings. Tele-nutrition is most effective when it provides actionable service linkage prescriptions rather than vague recommendations. These prescriptions should resemble medication orders and include specific details such as the destination facility or service, planned date and time, required documents, and verification at the next tele-follow-up. For children with suspected or confirmed Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM), tele-nutrition must identify the nearest NRC capable of stabilization, specify the planned date and time for the visit, and instruct caregivers to carry essential items such as the child’s health record, recent weight measurements, lab reports, and referral notes. Post-discharge follow-up should adhere to program norms, including frequent early contacts, weight monitoring, and linkage to community nutrition support. For children with moderate undernutrition or growth faltering, families eligible for supplementary nutrition and growth monitoring should be directed to their local Anganwadi center. Tele-nutrition prescriptions must document the center’s name and location, along with the scheduled day, often aligned with Village Health and Nutrition Days. Completion of these visits should be verified during subsequent tele-follow-ups. For school-age children, structured screening and early interventions under Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK) facilitate referrals for nutrition-related concerns identified during tele-visits. Families often possess program-side records, and the Poshan Tracker initiative supports beneficiary management and monitoring for Anganwadi services. Tele-nutrition teams can request screenshots or relevant records with consent to reconcile growth data and minimize duplication. To operationalize these prescriptions, tele-notes should include a dedicated section with four fields: destination facility or service, planned date, documents to carry, and verification date at follow-up. This structured approach enhances service continuity and ensures that children receive timely and appropriate nutrition-related interventions.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CONSENT, GUARDIAN VERIFICATION, AND MEDIA HANDLING IN PEDIATRIC TELE-CONSULTS
subtopics.category: ETHICS, COMMUNICATION CHANNELS, AND EQUITY IN INDIAN TELE-NUTRITION
subtopics.concept: Consent, guardian verification, and media handling are critical components of pediatric tele-consults, particularly in tele-nutrition. For minors, consent must be explicitly obtained from a verified parent, adult sibling, or legally appointed guardian, and the caregiver’s identity and relationship to the child must be documented in the tele-note. Additionally, the tele-note should record who was present during the consultation, as family dynamics and custody disputes can pose medico-legal risks. If authorization is unclear, disputed, or if a caregiver seeks records or advice without clear rights, the consultation should be paused until proper documentation is provided or a joint call with verified guardians is arranged. Safeguarding principles must be prioritized, and if concerns regarding child safety, such as neglect, abuse, or caregiver incapacity, are suspected, the clinician should escalate the issue according to local safeguarding protocols rather than continuing routine nutrition counseling. Media handling in pediatric tele-nutrition presents unique risks due to the clinical value of feeding photos and videos. Any recording, screenshots, or storage of such media should occur only with explicit documented consent. It is essential to specify where files are uploaded, who can access them, and when they will be deleted. Transparency regarding fees is also integral to consent; the consultation fee, what it includes (e.g., follow-up messages), and the protocol for early termination due to urgent referral should be clearly documented. A standardized consent line for tele-notes can streamline documentation: “Caregiver initiated/clinic initiated; consent implied/explicit; guardian name and relationship verified; photos/videos exchanged yes/no; media consent obtained yes/no; limitations of tele-assessment explained.” To ensure compliance, tele-note templates should include four mandatory checkboxes: consent documented, guardian verified, media consent documented, and tele-appropriate versus referred. Practical bedside application of these principles requires vigilance in verifying guardianship, safeguarding child welfare, and adhering to ethical media handling practices. Clinicians must remain alert to medico-legal risks and prioritize transparency and documentation to uphold patient safety and trust in pediatric tele-nutrition consultations.

case_studies.title: Consent and Guardian Verification in Pediatric Tele-Nutrition
case_studies.vignette: A 5-year-old boy from a semi-urban area in India is brought to a tele-nutrition consult by his maternal aunt, who states she is the child's primary caregiver due to the parents working abroad. The aunt reports concerns about the child's persistent poor appetite and weight loss over the past 3 months, with occasional loose stools. She shares a video of the child eating meals and requests advice on dietary modifications. Upon reviewing the tele-note, the clinician notices the aunt has not provided explicit documentation of guardianship or consent to act on behalf of the parents. The aunt insists she has verbal authorization from the parents but cannot provide written proof. During the consult, the clinician asks about the child's living conditions and notes potential signs of neglect in the video, such as unhygienic surroundings. The clinician pauses the consult and explains that proper verification of guardianship and consent is required before proceeding further. Additionally, the clinician advises the aunt of the need to escalate concerns about the child’s safety to appropriate local authorities if documentation or parental involvement cannot be established.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the importance of obtaining explicit consent and verifying guardianship in pediatric tele-nutrition consults, and recognize when to escalate concerns about child safety or caregiver incapacity.

subtopics.subtopic_title: DATA HYGIENE, RECORD RETENTION, AND COMMUNICATION CHANNEL SEPARATION
subtopics.category: ETHICS, COMMUNICATION CHANNELS, AND EQUITY IN INDIAN TELE-NUTRITION
subtopics.concept: Tele-nutrition in paediatrics involves the handling of sensitive child health data, such as growth measurements, photos, feeding videos, and laboratory reports, which are at risk of being lost or misused if stored on personal devices or shared via informal messaging apps. To ensure data hygiene, it is essential to separate communication channels from medical records. One channel should be designated for scheduling and instructions, another for synchronous consultations (video or audio), and a controlled channel should be used for storing clinical documents and media. If common messaging apps are used for practical reasons, sensitive data sharing should be minimized, explicit consent must be obtained, and all critical information must be transferred into the clinical record on the same day. Record retention should be deliberate and planned, adhering to telemedicine guidelines that mandate maintaining case records and digital trails, such as phone logs, emails, chat/text interactions, and video consultation logs, for at least three years from the last consultation. Child data protection principles also emphasize data minimization and purpose-limited retention, requiring clinics to establish a retention schedule that complies with medical record standards while enabling the deletion or erasure of data no longer needed, unless other legal requirements dictate otherwise. Operational protocols must be clearly defined. Clinics should enforce a strict “no media on personal gallery” policy, implement role-based access for team members, and utilize audit logs when available. Procedures for addressing lost devices or data breaches should be outlined, specifying who must be notified and what documentation is required. Consent processes should include a standard statement about the limitations of remote assessments. For practical implementation, clinics should create a concise “digital hygiene” policy that specifies where media is uploaded, who has access, the duration of data retention, and the requirement for same-day transfer of information from chat platforms to clinical records. These measures are critical to safeguarding sensitive paediatric data and ensuring ethical tele-nutrition practices.

subtopics.subtopic_title: LOW-BANDWIDTH CHANNEL TIERS AND SAFE USE OF MESSAGING PLATFORMS IN INDIA
subtopics.category: ETHICS, COMMUNICATION CHANNELS, AND EQUITY IN INDIAN TELE-NUTRITION
subtopics.concept: In the context of Indian tele-nutrition, selecting appropriate communication channels is crucial for ensuring safety, privacy, and reliable follow-up in paediatric care. Informal practices like using WhatsApp should be replaced with a structured, tiered channel policy that aligns with family capabilities while maintaining clinical standards. The tiered approach includes four levels: Tier 1 involves clinic platform messaging combined with video communication, used when available. Tier 2 relies on video or audio communication on standard platforms, with clinical documents uploaded exclusively through controlled links or portals. Tier 3 prioritizes audio-first care supplemented by asynchronous photo uploads of essential artifacts such as scale readings, meal photos, or lab reports. Tier 4 is reserved for low-tech care, consisting of phone counseling and scheduled in-person measurement points when digital artifacts cannot be safely exchanged. Across all tiers, safeguards must be implemented to protect sensitive data. Caregiver phone numbers should be verified at every episode of care to prevent misdirected messages, particularly in households with shared phones. Scheduling chats must be separated from clinical documentation, with all decisions and plans transferred to the medical record on the same day. Explicit consent is required before receiving child images or feeding videos via consumer messaging platforms, in compliance with child-data obligations under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023. Child media must not be stored in personal galleries but uploaded to a clinic repository with role-based access and audit logs, when available. Messaging should remain concise and task-oriented, such as requesting a weight photo on a specific day, while avoiding stigmatizing language in weight-management notifications. To facilitate practical application, institutions should publish a one-page channel policy for staff, detailing the permitted tiers, the types of data exchange allowed within each tier, and the requirement for same-day transfer of clinical information into the medical record. These measures ensure ethical, equitable, and safe use of messaging platforms in paediatric tele-nutrition in India.

case_studies.title: Tele-Nutrition in a Low-Bandwidth Setting: Safe Messaging Practices
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy from a rural village in India is referred to a tele-nutrition program due to poor weight gain over the last 6 months. His mother reports difficulty accessing healthcare due to long travel distances and limited internet connectivity. Over the past two weeks, she has noticed reduced appetite and intermittent irritability. A focused remote consultation is initiated using Tier 4 communication (phone counseling), as the family has no access to smartphones or stable internet. During the call, the mother describes his meals and offers a recent weight measurement from a local anganwadi center, which she claims was 7.2 kg. She is unsure of the accuracy of the scale used. The clinician requests explicit consent to schedule an in-person weight check at the nearest primary health center within the next week. No photos or videos are exchanged during the call. The mother’s phone number is verified twice during the session to ensure secure follow-up.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MINIMUM CYBERSECURITY BASELINE CONTROLS FOR PEDIATRIC TELE-NUTRITION PLATFORMS
subtopics.category: ETHICS, COMMUNICATION CHANNELS, AND EQUITY IN INDIAN TELE-NUTRITION
subtopics.concept: Pediatric tele-nutrition platforms handle sensitive child-specific data, including growth metrics, feeding videos, photos, and laboratory reports, often accessed via personal devices. To ensure privacy and safety are not reliant on individual staff behavior, a minimum cybersecurity baseline must be established with explicit controls. Identity and access management should include unique user accounts, strong passwords, multi-factor authentication (where feasible), and role-based access tailored to roles such as coordinators, dietitians, and registered medical practitioners (RMPs). Data security must involve encryption both in transit (TLS) and at rest, alongside a clinic policy mandating that files be uploaded to a secure repository rather than stored on personal devices. Device hygiene is critical, necessitating a bring-your-own-device (BYOD) policy that enforces screen locks, encrypted devices, and rapid deactivation of accounts on lost phones. Auditability should be ensured through access logs for records and media downloads, with periodic reviews conducted. To reduce the attack surface, data minimization practices should be employed, collecting only essential information, enforcing media retention timers, and ensuring operating systems and applications are updated with the latest security patches. Staff training on phishing and misdirected messaging is essential, particularly in low-resource settings where such vulnerabilities are common. Operational resilience must be prioritized by maintaining regular data backups, defining downtime workflows (e.g., paper or spreadsheet registries with later uploads), and conducting periodic restoration tests to safeguard records during ransomware attacks or device failures. Vendor assurance is a critical aspect of tool selection, requiring documented security controls, breach notification clauses, and the ability to export data for continuity. For practical implementation, a one-page security checklist should be used during onboarding and revisited quarterly. This checklist should verify accounts, access roles, encryption, backup success, patch updates, and audit log reviews, with completion marked as yes/no. These measures align with CERT-In Directions and FAQs on cybersecurity (2022) and relevant Indian guidelines, ensuring robust protection for pediatric tele-nutrition platforms.
